cards.back_html_sanitized:
To protect the brain from infections and blood-borne toxins by restricting the passage of large molecules.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A waste-clearance system in the brain that uses CSF to flush out cellular debris and proteins.

By using a rotating X-ray source to measure tissue density from multiple angles.

It uses powerful magnets and radio waves to track the relaxation of protons (hydrogen atoms).

White matter tracts (axonal fiber bundles).

By detecting radioactive glucose (or other chemicals) injected into the bloodstream.
Active brain regions use more glucose; the PET scanner detects the radiation emitted by these active areas during a task.

Blood-Oxygen-Level-Dependent signal, reflecting changes in blood flow and oxygen use.
fMRI detects small changes in metabolism (oxygen use) to identify which brain regions are active during specific cognitive tasks.

It has much better temporal resolution, tracking rapid, moment-by-moment changes in activity.
MEG uses SQUIDs to detect the tiny magnetic fields produced by active neurons in real-time.

Posterior cerebral arteries.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
An image produced by subtracting control condition values from experimental stimulation values.

The average of difference images obtained from multiple individuals in an experiment.
Averaging scans from several participants yields more reliable results across a population, though it may lose individual specificity.

The level of fine structural detail provided by an imaging technology.
fMRI has high spatial resolution, meaning it provides a very detailed map of where brain activity is occurring.

Techniques like EEG and MEG have high temporal resolution because they can track neural changes in milliseconds.

The inverse relationship between high spatial resolution and high temporal resolution.
Currently, no single technique offers both highly detailed images (accuracy/spatial) and extremely fast tracking (speed/temporal).

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The study of brain mechanisms that are both the cause and consequence of social behaviors.

Anterograde moves substances away from the cell body; retrograde moves them toward the cell body.

A restrictive barrier formed by blood vessel walls in the brain to protect against harmful molecules.

Approximately -50 to -80 millivolts (mV).

Anions are negatively charged ions; cations are positively charged ions.

Diffusion moves ions down concentration gradients; electrostatic pressure moves ions toward opposite charges.

The property of the cell membrane that allows only certain ions, like Potassium (K+), to pass through freely.

Three protective membranes that surround the brain and spinal cord.

Approximately -65 mV.
The interior of the neuron is more negative than the outside, creating a baseline level of polarization when the cell is not active.

Potassium (K+), Sodium (Na+), Chloride (Cl-), and Calcium (Ca2+).
The unequal distribution of these ions across the semipermeable membrane creates the electrical potential necessary for signaling.

It pumps three sodium (Na+) ions out of the cell for every two potassium (K+) ions it pumps in.
This energy-intensive mechanism maintains the high extracellular concentration of Na+ and high intracellular concentration of K+.

The property of a membrane that allows some substances (like K+) to pass through while blocking others (like Na+ at rest).
At rest, the membrane has open K+ channels but closed Na+ channels, making it more permeable to potassium.

Cations are attracted to the negative interior of the cell, while anions are repelled by it.

It calculates the voltage needed to exactly counterbalance the diffusion of a specific ion based on its concentration gradient.

It accounts for multiple ions (Na+, K+, Cl-) and their relative membrane permeabilities.
The Nernst equation only considers one ion, whereas real membranes are somewhat permeable to several different ions simultaneously.

Hyperpolarization moves the membrane potential further from zero, making it harder for the neuron to fire.

When the membrane is depolarized to this level, voltage-gated Na+ channels open abruptly.

Information is encoded by the frequency (number) of action potentials, not their individual size.

They open at threshold to allow a rapid influx of Na+ ions, causing the spike.

A brief period immediately following an action potential when no amount of stimulation can trigger another spike.
During this time, voltage-gated Na+ channels are inactivated and cannot be reopened.

This is due to the hyperpolarization caused by K+ ions flowing out of the cell, requiring more depolarization to reach threshold.

A technique used to study the currents of single ion channels.
A microelectrode tip forms a seal with a tiny patch of the cell membrane, allowing researchers to monitor individual channel openings.

They are actively regenerated along the length of the axon through ionic mechanisms.
Unlike passive graded potentials, the action potential maintains its amplitude as it travels by constantly reopening voltage-gated channels.

They represent the electrical oscillations and return to baseline following the action potential spike.

Its large size (0.5 mm) allowed early researchers to insert electrodes and measure electrical activity easily.

It uses electrodes to inject current into a neuron to keep the membrane potential at a set value.

Larger axons allow for faster spread of depolarization through the interior.

It selectively blocks voltage-gated sodium (Na+) channels, preventing the production of action potentials.

A local depolarizing potential that pushes the postsynaptic cell closer to the threshold for firing an action potential.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A hyperpolarizing potential that moves the membrane potential away from threshold, decreasing the chance of firing.
IPSPs often result from the entry of negative chloride ions (Cl-) or the exit of positive potassium ions (K+).

cards.front_text: What is an Inhibitory Postsynaptic Potential (IPSP)?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The time required for neurotransmitter release and diffusion across the synaptic cleft.
This delay, usually about 0.5 milliseconds, is the time between the arrival of an action potential and the start of the postsynaptic potential.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Spatial summation combines inputs from different locations; temporal summation combines inputs arriving at different times.
Both processes occur at the axon hillock to determine if the total depolarization reaches the threshold for an action potential.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It enters the axon terminal upon depolarization and triggers the fusion of synaptic vesicles with the membrane.
Without the influx of Ca2+ through voltage-gated channels, neurotransmitters cannot be released into the synaptic cleft.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Enzymatic degradation and reuptake via transporters.
Removal ensures that the signal is brief and that the synapse is ready for the next message.
cards.back_text: Enzymatic degradation and reuptake via transporters. Removal ensures that the signal is brief and that the synapse is ready for the next message.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A receptor on the presynaptic membrane that monitors and regulates future neurotransmitter release.
It acts as a feedback mechanism to tell the presynaptic neuron how much transmitter has already been released into the cleft.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The fact that the size (amplitude) of the action potential is independent of the stimulus size.
Once threshold is reached, the neuron fires a full-sized action potential; if threshold is not reached, it does not fire at all.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
They use voltage-gated ion channels to sum and amplify local postsynaptic potentials before they reach the axon hillock.
This allows distant synapses on the dendrites to have a greater impact on whether the neuron eventually fires.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Calcium (Ca2+)
When an action potential reaches the terminal, it opens voltage-gated calcium channels. The influx of Ca2+ ions then activates enzymes that cause synaptic vesicles to fuse with the membrane.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Exocytosis
Exocytosis is the final step of transmitter release where the vesicle membrane incorporates into the presynaptic membrane, dumping its contents into the synaptic cleft.
cards.back_text: Exocytosis Exocytosis is the final step of transmitter release where the vesicle membrane incorporates into the presynaptic membrane, dumping its contents into the synaptic cleft.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
v-SNAREs (on the vesicle) and t-SNAREs (on the target/presynaptic membrane).
These proteins act like tethers; when they attach to each other, the vesicle becomes 'docked' and ready for release.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Synaptotagmin
Synaptotagmin is attached to the vesicle and, when bound by incoming calcium ions, catalyzes the fusion of the vesicular and presynaptic membranes.
cards.back_text: Synaptotagmin Synaptotagmin is attached to the vesicle and, when bound by incoming calcium ions, catalyzes the fusion of the vesicular and presynaptic membranes.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
They cut up (cleave) SNARE proteins.
By destroying SNARE proteins, these toxins disable the exocytosis process, effectively silencing the synapse because neurotransmitters cannot be released.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Endogenous ligands are made inside the body (e.g., neurotransmitters); exogenous ligands come from outside (e.g., drugs).
Both types can bind to receptors to either activate or block them, similar to a lock-and-key mechanism.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
An agonist mimics a transmitter's action; an antagonist blocks or prevents the transmitter's action.
Agonists struggle 'for' the response, while antagonists interfere with the binding of the natural ligand.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It proved that neural communication involves chemical signals, not just electrical ones.
By transferring fluid from a stimulated heart to a non-stimulated one and seeing the same effect, Loewi identified 'Vagusstoff' (later known as acetylcholine).

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Ionotropic receptors are fast; metabotropic receptors are slow.
Ionotropic receptors are ligand-gated ion channels that open immediately. Metabotropic receptors use G proteins and second messengers, leading to a delay but more amplification.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It amplifies and prolongs the synaptic signal inside the postsynaptic cell.
Activated by G proteins, second messengers like cyclic AMP (cAMP) can initiate wide-ranging biochemical changes within the neuron.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Degradation and Reuptake.
Degradation involves enzymes breaking down the transmitter (e.g., AChE), while reuptake involves 'transporters' bringing the transmitter back into the presynaptic terminal.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It monitors the concentration of released neurotransmitter and regulates future release.
Located on the presynaptic membrane, it provides a feedback loop to tell the axon terminal if it should release more or less transmitter.
cards.back_text: It monitors the concentration of released neurotransmitter and regulates future release. Located on the presynaptic membrane, it provides a feedback loop to tell the axon terminal if it should release more or less transmitter.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Up-regulation is an increase in receptor density; down-regulation is a decrease in receptor density.
These are compensatory changes in sensitivity, often occurring in response to drug use or physiological changes.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It allows ions to flow directly between cells through 'connexons' with almost no time delay.
Unlike the 20-40nm gap in chemical synapses, electrical synapses are only 2-4nm apart and allow for synchronized firing.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A synapse where a presynaptic axon terminal connects to another axon terminal.
This allows one neuron to directly regulate the amount of neurotransmitter released by another neuron's terminal.
cards.back_text: A synapse where a presynaptic axon terminal connects to another axon terminal. This allows one neuron to directly regulate the amount of neurotransmitter released by another neuron's terminal.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A signal (usually a gas like NO) flows from the postsynaptic neuron back to the presynaptic neuron.
This reverse signaling tells the presynaptic cell to adjust its transmitter release, counter to the usual direction of communication.
cards.back_text: A signal (usually a gas like NO) flows from the postsynaptic neuron back to the presynaptic neuron. This reverse signaling tells the presynaptic cell to adjust its transmitter release, counter to the usual direction of communication.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A process where a vesicle releases its contents briefly then pinches off to return to the terminal without fully merging.
This is a variation of exocytosis that allows for faster recycling of synaptic vesicles.
cards.back_text: A process where a vesicle releases its contents briefly then pinches off to return to the terminal without fully merging. This is a variation of exocytosis that allows for faster recycling of synaptic vesicles.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A simple neural circuit where neurons are attached linearly, end to end.
The neural chain is the most straightforward link of neurons in the nervous system, such as those found in basic reflex arcs.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A sensory neuron, a motor neuron, and a single synapse.
This reflex is extremely rapid (about 40ms) because it involves large myelinated axons and a direct monosynaptic connection.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A phenomenon where many neurons send signals to a single neuron.
In the eye, for example, about 100 million receptor cells concentrate their information onto about 1 million optic nerve axons.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A phenomenon where one cell sends signals to many other cells.
In the visual system, information from the optic nerve diverges to influence billions of neurons across different specialized regions of the cerebral cortex.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A recording of gross electrical activity of the brain via electrodes on the scalp.
EEGs detect spontaneous brain waves and are used to study sleep stages and diagnose seizure disorders.

Widespread synchronization of electrical activity across the brain.
While normal brain activity is desynchronized, seizures involve broad swaths of neurons firing in simultaneous waves of excitation.

Loss of consciousness followed by enduring muscle contraction and then rhythmic jerking.
It involves symmetrical involvement of body musculature and abnormal EEG activity across the entire brain.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A brief spike-and-wave EEG episode causing a loss of awareness without unusual muscle activity.
The person often stares blankly for 5-15 seconds and has no memory of the event afterward.

An unusual sensation or premonition that precedes a complex partial seizure.
Auras can manifest as unusual abdominal sensations, a sense of foreboding, or tingling in the limbs.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Repeated, weak electrical stimulation that eventually causes spontaneous seizures.
This suggests that repeated small electrical insults can permanently change brain tissue to make it more epilepsy-prone.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Averaged EEG recordings measuring brain responses to discrete stimuli.
By averaging many trials, the background noise is removed to reveal specific wave shapes associated with sensory processing.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Short-latency responses reflect physical stimulus characteristics; long-latency responses reflect cognitive processing.
Early waves (brainstem) are influenced by stimulus intensity, while later waves reflect attention and decision-making.

Using genetic tools to insert light-sensitive ion channels into neurons to control their activity with light.
This allows researchers to excite or inhibit specific types of neurons with millisecond precision using fiber-optic cables.

A light-sensitive protein that depolarizes neurons when exposed to blue light.

A light-sensitive protein that hyperpolarizes neurons when exposed to yellow light.
Yellow light causes the pump to move Cl- ions into the cell, making it more negative and preventing it from firing.

The opening of voltage-gated sodium (Na+) channels.

The jumping of action potentials from one Node of Ranvier to the next along a myelinated axon.

Spatial sums potentials from different locations; temporal sums potentials arriving at different times.
The neuron integrates these inputs at the axon hillock to determine if the threshold for an action potential is reached.

A brief time after an action potential when the neuron cannot be excited by any stimulus.
This is due to the voltage-gated sodium channels being inactivated, ensuring the signal only moves in one direction.

Ionotropic receptors are ion channels; metabotropic receptors use G proteins to trigger second messengers.

Exogenous substances originate from outside the body, while endogenous substances are produced internally.
Drugs and toxins are considered exogenous, whereas neurotransmitters produced by the brain are endogenous.

Calcium (Ca2+) ions.
When an action potential reaches the terminal, it opens voltage-gated calcium channels; the resulting influx of Ca2+ triggers vesicle fusion and exocytosis.

They facilitate the fusion of synaptic vesicles with the presynaptic membrane.
SNAREs are large proteins on both the vesicle and the presynaptic membrane that 'zip' together to allow the release of neurotransmitters into the synaptic cleft.

Ionotropic receptors are fast-acting ligand-gated ion channels; metabotropic receptors are slower and use second messenger systems.
Ionotropic receptors change shape to open a channel immediately upon binding, while metabotropic receptors activate G proteins that indirectly influence the cell.

A specific version of a receptor for a single neurotransmitter that has unique functional and anatomical characteristics.

Glutamate and Aspartate.

The medial septal nucleus, the nucleus of the diagonal band, and the nucleus basalis.

Nicotinic and Muscarinic receptors.

Dopamine, Epinephrine, and Norepinephrine.

The mesostriatal pathway and the mesolimbocortical pathway.

The mesostriatal pathway (specifically neurons in the substantia nigra).

In the raphe nuclei of the midbrain and brainstem.

To break down and inactivate monoamine neurotransmitters (dopamine, serotonin, and norepinephrine).

The presence and release of more than one type of neurotransmitter from a single presynaptic terminal.

It rapidly breaks down acetylcholine (ACh) in the synaptic cleft.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The mesostriatal pathway originates in the substantia nigra and projects to the striatum (caudate and putamen).
This dopaminergic pathway is crucial for motor control and is significantly impacted in conditions like Parkinson's disease.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The raphe nuclei.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The locus coeruleus is located in the pons (part of the brainstem) and produces norepinephrine (NE).
Often called the 'blue spot', it provides broad noradrenergic innervation to the cerebrum, limbic system, and thalamus, influencing arousal and mood.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
They are produced outside axon terminals (often in dendrites), are not stored in vesicles, and act as retrograde transmitters by diffusing into the target cell.
Classic transmitters are stored in vesicles and released from the presynaptic terminal. Gas transmitters diffuse immediately upon production and can signal backwards from the postsynaptic to the presynaptic neuron.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A neurotransmitter that diffuses from the postsynaptic neuron back to the presynaptic neuron.
Retrograde signaling is often used to modulate synaptic efficacy and is heavily involved in mechanisms of learning and memory.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Affinity is the degree of chemical attraction (how well it binds), while efficacy (intrinsic activity) is the ability of the bound ligand to activate the receptor.
A drug can have high affinity (binds strongly) but low efficacy (doesn't activate the receptor), which characterizes an antagonist.

A drug can have high affinity (binds strongly) but low efficacy (doesn't activate the receptor), which characterizes an antagonist.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A competitive ligand binds to the same site as the endogenous transmitter, while a noncompetitive ligand binds to a separate modulatory site.
Noncompetitive ligands (or neuromodulators) do not directly compete for the primary binding site but can still activate or block the receptor's function.
cards.back_text: A competitive ligand binds to the same site as the endogenous transmitter, while a noncompetitive ligand binds to a separate modulatory site. cards.back_html_sanitized:
An inverse agonist binds to a receptor and initiates an effect that is the opposite of the normal function of that receptor.
While an antagonist simply blocks action, an inverse agonist induces a functional response that is the reverse of the endogenous ligand's effect.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The margin of safety determined by the separation between the effective dose (ED50) and the lethal dose (LD50).
A wider therapeutic index indicates a safer drug because there is a large difference between the dose that treats symptoms and the dose that is toxic.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Pharmacodynamics is the study of how a drug affects the body (targets), while pharmacokinetics is the study of how the body moves the drug through its systems (absorption, metabolism).
Pharmacokinetics involves the 'journey' of the drug (ingestion to excretion), whereas pharmacodynamics involves the 'action' at the receptor level.

cards.front_text: What is the difference between pharmacodynamics and pharmacokinetics?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Metabolic tolerance and functional tolerance.
Metabolic tolerance involves more efficient clearance by organs like the liver. Functional tolerance involves changes in receptor density (up-regulation or down-regulation).
cards.back_text: Metabolic tolerance and functional tolerance. Metabolic tolerance involves more efficient clearance by organs like the liver. Functional tolerance involves changes in receptor density (up-regulation or down-regulation).

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Down-regulation is the decrease in the number of available receptors on a neuron, often occurring in response to repeated exposure to an agonist.
The cell compensates for overstimulation by removing receptors from the membrane, which contributes to functional tolerance.
cards.back_text: Down-regulation is the decrease in the number of available receptors on a neuron, often occurring in response to repeated exposure to an agonist. The cell compensates for overstimulation by removing receptors from the membrane, which contributes to functional tolerance.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Cross-tolerance is when the development of tolerance for one drug generalizes to other drugs in the same chemical class.
For example, tolerance to heroin can lead to tolerance for other opioids like morphine because they act on the same receptor systems.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Norepinephrine (NE), also known as noradrenaline.
Norepinephrine is crucial for arousal in the brain and is released by sympathetic postganglionic neurons to accelerate organ activity.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Opioid peptides are endogenous substances that mimic morphine; examples include endorphins, enkephalins, and dynorphins.
These peptides bind to specific receptors (mu, delta, kappa) to produce analgesia (pain relief) and reward.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A compensatory reduction in the number of receptors available at a synapse.
When an agonist drug over-stimulates a neuron, the postsynaptic cell may decrease its receptor count to become less sensitive, moving back toward a pre-drug state.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A compensatory increase in the number of receptors at a synapse.
This typically occurs in response to an antagonist drug; the cell adds receptors to become more sensitive and compensate for the blocked endogenous signals.
cards.back_text: A compensatory increase in the number of receptors at a synapse. This typically occurs in response to an antagonist drug; the cell adds receptors to become more sensitive and compensate for the blocked endogenous signals.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Metabolic tolerance involves efficient drug clearance, while functional tolerance involves changes in receptor sensitivity.
In metabolic tolerance, the body's machinery (like the liver) gets better at eliminating the drug. In functional tolerance, the brain alters its own receptor density (up- or down-regulation).

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Developing tolerance for one drug as a result of developing tolerance for another related drug.
If two drugs affect the same chemical system, like different types of opiates, tolerance to one often transfers to the other.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A process where the body shows an enhanced response to a drug after repeated doses.
Unlike tolerance, sensitization makes the individual more sensitive, often contributing to drug cravings and long-term changes in the dopamine reward pathway.

By blocking sodium (Na+) channels in axons.

A presynaptic receptor that monitors and regulates the amount of transmitter released.
It acts as a feedback system; when the autoreceptor is triggered, it usually tells the presynaptic neuron to slow down transmitter release.

It blocks adenosine receptors on the presynaptic membrane.
Adenosine normally inhibits transmitter release. By blocking these receptors, caffeine prevents that inhibition, leading to increased catecholamine release and arousal.

They are proteins that return neurotransmitter molecules to the presynaptic neuron for reuse.

By blocking the reuptake of serotonin, allowing it to accumulate in the synapse.
By keeping serotonin in the synaptic cleft longer, these drugs enhance the transmitter's effect on the postsynaptic cell.

Selective antagonism of dopamine D2 receptors.

It cuts SNARE proteins, preventing the release of acetylcholine (ACh).

Delta, kappa, and mu; they are all metabotropic receptors.

They act as powerful opiate antagonists that block opioid receptors.

A receptor for which no endogenous ligand has yet been discovered.

CB1 is found in the nervous system; CB2 is prominent in the immune system.

An endogenous substance (endocannabinoid) that binds to cannabinoid receptors.

Nicotinic acetylcholine (ACh) receptors.

It blocks monoamine transporters, especially for dopamine.

Inducing release without action potentials, potentiating release during action potentials, blocking reuptake, and inhibiting metabolic breakdown (MAO).

By activating the GABA-A receptor-coupled chloride channel.

A disorder resulting from maternal alcohol ingestion during pregnancy, causing intellectual disability and facial anomalies.

Because they tend to alter or distort existing perceptions rather than create entirely novel ones.

Steroids produced directly within the brain.

The 5-HT2A receptor.

It reduces activity in the medial prefrontal cortex and anterior cingulate cortex, disinhibiting the limbic system.

Up-regulation increases the number of receptors; down-regulation decreases them.

Chemical signals (hormones) released into the bloodstream to affect distant targets.

Pheromones act within a species; allomones act between different species.

Hormones are released in brief, rhythmic bursts rather than a constant flow.

The output of the hormone feeds back to inhibit the drive for more of that same hormone.

Extracellular senile plaques (beta-amyloid) and intracellular neurofibrillary tangles (Tau).

cards.back_text: Extracellular senile plaques (beta-amyloid) and intracellular neurofibrillary tangles (Tau). The buildup of these abnormal protein aggregates leads to the degeneration of neurons throughout the cortex.
cards.front_text: What are the two primary pathological markers of Alzheimer's disease?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The specific type of energy for which a given sensory organ is particularly adapted.
For example, light is the adequate stimulus for the eye, while sound vibrations are the adequate stimulus for the ear.
cards.back_text: The specific type of energy for which a given sensory organ is particularly adapted. For example, light is the adequate stimulus for the eye, while sound vibrations are the adequate stimulus for the ear.
cards.front_text: What is an adequate stimulus?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The idea that each nerve input to the brain reports only a particular type of information because the tracts are dedicated to specific senses.
The brain distinguishes between different modalities (like touch versus sound) based on which neural pathway carries the action potentials.
cards.back_text: The idea that each nerve input to the brain reports only a particular type of information because the tracts are dedicated to specific senses. The brain distinguishes between different modalities (like touch versus sound) based on which neural pathway carries the action potentials.
cards.front_text: What is the concept of labeled lines?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The process by which a receptor cell converts energy from a stimulus into a change in electrical potential.
Transduction is the critical first step in sensory processing where physical or chemical energy is translated into the electrical language of the nervous system.
cards.back_text: The process by which a receptor cell converts energy from a stimulus into a change in electrical potential. Transduction is the critical first step in sensory processing where physical or chemical energy is translated into the electrical language of the nervous system.
cards.front_text: What is sensory transduction?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A local, graded change in the resting potential of a receptor cell in response to a stimulus.
If this graded potential is large enough to reach the threshold, it will trigger one or more action potentials in the sensory neuron.
cards.back_text: A local, graded change in the resting potential of a receptor cell in response to a stimulus. If this graded potential is large enough to reach the threshold, it will trigger one or more action potentials in the sensory neuron.
cards.front_text: What is a receptor potential (or generator potential)?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Vibration and pressure.
This receptor, located in the skin and muscles, uses its onion-like layered structure to detect mechanical deformation.
cards.back_text: Vibration and pressure. This receptor, located in the skin and muscles, uses its onion-like layered structure to detect mechanical deformation.
cards.front_text: What specific stimulus does the Pacinian corpuscle detect?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A coding strategy where different receptor cells are specialized for different segments of an intensity scale.
This allows a collection of neurons with different thresholds to accurately represent a wide range of stimulus intensities.
cards.back_text: A coding strategy where different receptor cells are specialized for different segments of an intensity scale. This allows a collection of neurons with different thresholds to accurately represent a wide range of stimulus intensities.
cards.front_text: What is range fractionation?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The progressive loss of a receptor's response when a stimulus is maintained at a constant level.
Adaptation allows the nervous system to focus on new changes in the environment rather than continuous, unchanging background information.
cards.back_text: The progressive loss of a receptor's response when a stimulus is maintained at a constant level. Adaptation allows the nervous system to focus on new changes in the environment rather than continuous, unchanging background information.
cards.front_text: What is sensory adaptation?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Tonic receptors show little to no adaptation; phasic receptors adapt rapidly to maintained stimuli.
Tonic receptors provide constant information about a state, while phasic receptors are optimized for signaling changes.
cards.back_text: Tonic receptors show little to no adaptation; phasic receptors adapt rapidly to maintained stimuli. Tonic receptors provide constant information about a state, while phasic receptors are optimized for signaling changes.
cards.front_text: What is the difference between tonic and phasic receptors?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Environmental chemicals that influence the growth, survival, and differentiation of neurons.
These factors, along with nutrition and experience, are essential external influences on the developing and adult brain.
cards.back_text: Environmental chemicals that influence the growth, survival, and differentiation of neurons. These factors, along with nutrition and experience, are essential external influences on the developing and adult brain.
cards.front_text: What are neurotrophic factors?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It influences the risk of the disease and the rate at which beta-amyloid accumulates in the brain.
Variations in the apolipoprotein E (ApoE) gene are significant genetic risk factors for late-onset Alzheimer's.
cards.back_text: It influences the risk of the disease and the rate at which beta-amyloid accumulates in the brain. Variations in the apolipoprotein E (ApoE) gene are significant genetic risk factors for late-onset Alzheimer's.
cards.front_text: What role does the ApoE gene play in Alzheimer's disease?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The theory that each sensory modality uses independent neural channels that produce only one type of sensation.
Proposed by Johannes Müller, this states that the sensation depends on which nerve is stimulated, not how it is stimulated.
cards.back_text: The theory that each sensory modality uses independent neural channels that produce only one type of sensation. Proposed by Johannes Müller, this states that the sensation depends on which nerve is stimulated, not how it is stimulated.
cards.front_text: What is the doctrine of specific nerve energies?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Synapse rearrangement (or synaptic pruning).
Neural activity and experience determine which connections are maintained and which are lost, a process vital for the proper development of systems like vision.
cards.back_text: Synapse rearrangement (or synaptic pruning). Neural activity and experience determine which connections are maintained and which are lost, a process vital for the proper development of systems like vision.
cards.front_text: What process describes the elimination of some synapses and the survival of others based on experience?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A lack of functional pain receptors or pathways, despite being able to detect other sensations like touch.
Cases like Ashlyn B. demonstrate that pain is a distinct sensory modality necessary for survival, and its absence leads to unnoticed injuries.
cards.back_text: A lack of functional pain receptors or pathways, despite being able to detect other sensations like touch. Cases like Ashlyn B. demonstrate that pain is a distinct sensory modality necessary for survival, and its absence leads to unnoticed injuries.
cards.front_text: What causes the sensory experience in individuals who cannot feel pain?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Through maplike arrangements (topographic maps) where specific receptors connect to specific areas of the brain.
The spatial organization of receptors on the skin is preserved as information travels through labeled lines to the cerebral cortex.
cards.back_text: Through maplike arrangements (topographic maps) where specific receptors connect to specific areas of the brain. The spatial organization of receptors on the skin is preserved as information travels through labeled lines to the cerebral cortex.
cards.front_text: How is the location of a stimulus represented in the somatosensory system?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The progressive loss of receptor sensitivity as stimulation is maintained.
Sensory adaptation allows the nervous system to ignore constant, unchanging stimuli and prioritize environmental changes that are often more critical for survival.
cards.back_text: The progressive loss of receptor sensitivity as stimulation is maintained. Sensory adaptation allows the nervous system to ignore constant, unchanging stimuli and prioritize environmental changes that are often more critical for survival.
cards.front_text: What is sensory adaptation?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Tonic receptors show little or no decline in action potential frequency, while phasic receptors show a rapid drop.
Tonic receptors are essential for monitoring duration or intensity over time, whereas phasic receptors are specialized for detecting changes or the onset/offset of a stimulus.
cards.back_text: Tonic receptors show little or no decline in action potential frequency, while phasic receptors show a rapid drop. Tonic receptors are essential for monitoring duration or intensity over time, whereas phasic receptors are specialized for detecting changes or the onset/offset of a stimulus.
cards.front_text: What is the difference between tonic and phasic receptors?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Higher brain regions send descending axons to lower sensory stations to inhibit or enhance incoming signals.
This allows the brain to suppress irrelevant information, such as the pain system inhibiting incoming signals at the spinal cord level or the auditory system adjusting receptor sensitivity.
cards.back_text: Higher brain regions send descending axons to lower sensory stations to inhibit or enhance incoming signals. This allows the brain to suppress irrelevant information, such as the pain system inhibiting incoming signals at the spinal cord level or the auditory system adjusting receptor sensitivity.
cards.front_text: How does top-down processing modulate sensory input?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It acts as a relay station, trading information with the cortex for almost all sensory modalities except smell.
Each sensory modality (vision, hearing, touch) is sent to a specific division of the thalamus before reaching the cerebral cortex. The cortex can also direct the thalamus to suppress or emphasize specific information.
cards.back_text: It acts as a relay station, trading information with the cortex for almost all sensory modalities except smell. Each sensory modality (vision, hearing, touch) is sent to a specific division of the thalamus before reaching the cerebral cortex. The cortex can also direct the thalamus to suppress or emphasize specific information.
cards.front_text: What is the primary role of the thalamus in sensory processing?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A region of space in which a stimulus will alter that neuron's firing rate.
Receptive fields in the somatosensory system often have an excitatory center and an inhibitory surround (or vice versa), which helps the brain detect edges and discontinuities.
cards.back_text: A region of space in which a stimulus will alter that neuron's firing rate. Receptive fields in the somatosensory system often have an excitatory center and an inhibitory surround (or vice versa), which helps the brain detect edges and discontinuities.
cards.front_text: What defines a neuron's receptive field?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
In the postcentral gyrus of the parietal lobe, just behind the central sulcus.
S1 receives touch information from the opposite (contralateral) side of the body and is organized into a map of the body surface.
cards.back_text: In the postcentral gyrus of the parietal lobe, just behind the central sulcus. S1 receives touch information from the opposite (contralateral) side of the body and is organized into a map of the body surface.
cards.front_text: Where is the primary somatosensory cortex (S1) located?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A map of the body surface in the brain where the size of each area reflects the density of its sensory innervation.
Highly sensitive areas like the hands, fingers, and lips occupy much larger portions of the somatosensory cortex compared to less sensitive areas like the back or shoulders.
cards.back_text: A map of the body surface in the brain where the size of each area reflects the density of its sensory innervation. Highly sensitive areas like the hands, fingers, and lips occupy much larger portions of the somatosensory cortex compared to less sensitive areas like the back or shoulders.
cards.front_text: What does the 'sensory homunculus' represent?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The posterior parietal lobe and the cingulate cortex.
The posterior parietal lobe is crucial for selective awareness and expecting stimuli, while the cingulate cortex (specifically the anterior portion) is linked to the emotional aspects of stimuli like pain.
cards.back_text: The posterior parietal lobe and the cingulate cortex. The posterior parietal lobe is crucial for selective awareness and expecting stimuli, while the cingulate cortex (specifically the anterior portion) is linked to the emotional aspects of stimuli like pain.
cards.front_text: Which brain regions are most active during top-down attentional focus?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A condition where a stimulus in one modality evokes an involuntary sensation in another modality.
For example, a person may 'see' a specific color when they hear a musical note or 'taste' a flavor when they see a specific word. It is associated with increased axonal connections between brain regions.
cards.back_text: A condition where a stimulus in one modality evokes an involuntary sensation in another modality. For example, a person may 'see' a specific color when they hear a musical note or 'taste' a flavor when they see a specific word. It is associated with increased axonal connections between brain regions.
cards.front_text: What is synesthesia?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Epidermis, dermis, and hypodermis (subcutaneous tissue).
The epidermis is the thin outer layer, the dermis contains nerve fibers and blood vessels, and the hypodermis provides an anchor for muscles and contains larger receptors like Pacinian corpuscles.
cards.back_text: Epidermis, dermis, and hypodermis (subcutaneous tissue). The epidermis is the thin outer layer, the dermis contains nerve fibers and blood vessels, and the hypodermis provides an anchor for muscles and contains larger receptors like Pacinian corpuscles.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It detects vibration and rapid mechanical changes.
Found in the hypodermis, it is a fast-adapting receptor. Its onion-like structure filters out slow pressure, allowing only high-frequency vibrations (above 200 Hz) to reach the nerve fiber.
cards.back_text: It detects vibration and rapid mechanical changes. Found in the hypodermis, it is a fast-adapting receptor. Its onion-like structure filters out slow pressure, allowing only high-frequency vibrations (above 200 Hz) to reach the nerve fiber.
cards.front_text: What is the specific function of the Pacinian corpuscle?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Merkel’s discs and Meissner’s corpuscles.
Merkel’s discs are slow-adapting and provide high spatial resolution for form (like Braille), while Meissner’s corpuscles are fast-adapting and detect localized movement and texture.
cards.back_text: Merkel’s discs and Meissner’s corpuscles. Merkel’s discs are slow-adapting and provide high spatial resolution for form (like Braille), while Meissner’s corpuscles are fast-adapting and detect localized movement and texture.
cards.front_text: Which touch receptors are responsible for detecting form and fine detail?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Stretching of the skin.
These are slow-adapting receptors with large receptive fields that respond when we move our limbs or fingers, causing the skin to stretch.
cards.back_text: Stretching of the skin. These are slow-adapting receptors with large receptive fields that respond when we move our limbs or fingers, causing the skin to stretch.
cards.front_text: What do Ruffini’s endings detect?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Specialized ion channels that open in response to mechanical stretch to depolarize the cell.
These proteins are the molecular basis for light-touch sensation; when the cell membrane is stretched, the Piezo channel allows cations to enter, triggering an action potential.
cards.back_text: Specialized ion channels that open in response to mechanical stretch to depolarize the cell. These proteins are the molecular basis for light-touch sensation; when the cell membrane is stretched, the Piezo channel allows cations to enter, triggering an action potential.
cards.front_text: What are Piezo proteins?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A-beta (Aβ) fibers.
A-beta fibers are moderately large and myelinated, allowing for rapid conduction of tactile information compared to the smaller, slower fibers used for some types of pain and temperature.
cards.back_text: A-beta (Aβ) fibers. A-beta fibers are moderately large and myelinated, allowing for rapid conduction of tactile information compared to the smaller, slower fibers used for some types of pain and temperature.
cards.front_text: Which axon type carries light-touch information to the central nervous system?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
S1 receives the main input from the thalamus, while S2 receives input from S1 and maps both sides of the body in registered overlay.
S1 maps the opposite side of the body, whereas S2 integrates information from both sides, allowing for a more complex representation of touch.
cards.back_text: S1 receives the main input from the thalamus, while S2 receives input from S1 and maps both sides of the body in registered overlay. S1 maps the opposite side of the body, whereas S2 integrates information from both sides, allowing for a more complex representation of touch.
cards.front_text: What is the difference between primary (S1) and secondary (S2) somatosensory cortex?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Neurons that respond to input from more than one sensory modality.
These cells are found in association areas of the brain and allow for the integration of different senses, such as hearing and vision, to create a unified perception of an object.
cards.back_text: Neurons that respond to input from more than one sensory modality. These cells are found in association areas of the brain and allow for the integration of different senses, such as hearing and vision, to create a unified perception of an object.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
They physically reduce the intensity of a stimulus before it reaches the receptor.
Examples include closing eyelids to reduce light or contracting middle-ear muscles to dampen loud sounds, protecting the receptors from overstimulation.
cards.back_text: They physically reduce the intensity of a stimulus before it reaches the receptor. Examples include closing eyelids to reduce light or contracting middle-ear muscles to dampen loud sounds, protecting the receptors from overstimulation.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It enhances spatial resolution and the detection of edges.
By inhibiting the firing of a neuron when the area immediately surrounding its excitatory center is stimulated, the brain can more precisely locate the boundaries of a touch stimulus.
cards.back_text: It enhances spatial resolution and the detection of edges. By inhibiting the firing of a neuron when the area immediately surrounding its excitatory center is stimulated, the brain can more precisely locate the boundaries of a touch stimulus.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Smell (Olfaction).
Unlike vision, hearing, and touch, olfactory information travels from the receptors directly to the olfactory cortex without first being relayed through the thalamus.
cards.back_text: Smell (Olfaction). Unlike vision, hearing, and touch, olfactory information travels from the receptors directly to the olfactory cortex without first being relayed through the thalamus.
cards.front_text: Which sensory modality bypasses the thalamus?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It delivers most touch stimuli from the skin to the brain.
Axons from touch receptors enter the spinal cord, travel up the dorsal column of white matter, and synapse in the medulla.
cards.back_text: It delivers most touch stimuli from the skin to the brain. Axons from touch receptors enter the spinal cord, travel up the dorsal column of white matter, and synapse in the medulla.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
In the medulla.
Axons from the dorsal column nuclei in the medulla cross to the opposite side before ascending to the thalamus and somatosensory cortex.
cards.back_text: In the medulla. Axons from the dorsal column nuclei in the medulla cross to the opposite side before ascending to the thalamus and somatosensory cortex.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A region of skin innervated by a particular spinal nerve.
The skin surface is divided into bands that correspond to the spinal nerves; this pattern reflects our quadrupedal evolutionary history.
cards.back_text: A region of skin innervated by a particular spinal nerve. The skin surface is divided into bands that correspond to the spinal nerves; this pattern reflects our quadrupedal evolutionary history.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The ability of the brain's somatosensory regions to reorganize based on experience or injury.
Experiments show that if a finger is lost, the cortical area formerly representing it will expand to respond to adjacent fingers.
cards.back_text: The ability of the brain's somatosensory regions to reorganize based on experience or injury. Experiments show that if a finger is lost, the cortical area formerly representing it will expand to respond to adjacent fingers.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Withdrawal from danger, promotion of recuperative behaviors, and social signaling.
Pain forces us to move away from harm, encourages rest/healing, and signals distress to others to elicit care.
cards.back_text: Withdrawal from danger, promotion of recuperative behaviors, and social signaling. Pain forces us to move away from harm, encourages rest/healing, and signals distress to others to elicit care.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Sensory-discriminative, motivational-affective, and cognitive-evaluative.
These categories describe the physical quality of the pain, the emotional reaction to it, and the overall assessment of its severity.
cards.back_text: Sensory-discriminative, motivational-affective, and cognitive-evaluative. These categories describe the physical quality of the pain, the emotional reaction to it, and the overall assessment of its severity.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A specialized peripheral receptor that responds to stimuli that produce tissue damage.
Nociceptors are found on free nerve endings and detect pain or extreme temperature changes.
cards.back_text: A specialized peripheral receptor that responds to stimuli that produce tissue damage. Nociceptors are found on free nerve endings and detect pain or extreme temperature changes.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It detects moderate heat and the chemical capsaicin from chili peppers.
TRPV1 is a member of the TRP ion channel family; it is why spicy food tastes 'hot'—the brain interprets the signal as heat.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
TRPM3 detects higher temperatures and is found on larger, faster A delta fibers.
TRPM3 reports sharp, immediate pain from high heat, whereas TRPV1 signals a slower, duller ache.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
They are large-diameter, myelinated, and fast-conducting axons.
These fibers transmit the initial, sharp pain felt immediately after an injury.
cards.back_text: They are large-diameter, myelinated, and fast-conducting axons. These fibers transmit the initial, sharp pain felt immediately after an injury.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
They are small, unmyelinated, slow-conducting axons.
C fibers produce the dull, lasting ache that follows the initial sharp pain of an injury.
cards.back_text: They are small, unmyelinated, slow-conducting axons. C fibers produce the dull, lasting ache that follows the initial sharp pain of an injury.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It responds to cool temperatures and the chemical menthol.
Also known as cool-menthol receptor 1 (CMR1), it is found on C fibers and signals cooling sensations.
cards.back_text: It responds to cool temperatures and the chemical menthol. Also known as cool-menthol receptor 1 (CMR1), it is found on C fibers and signals cooling sensations.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It encodes a specific voltage-gated sodium channel used by nociceptors to initiate action potentials.
People with mutations in this gene cannot feel pain (congenital insensitivity to pain) because their pain receptors cannot fire.
cards.back_text: It encodes a specific voltage-gated sodium channel used by nociceptors to initiate action potentials. People with mutations in this gene cannot feel pain (congenital insensitivity to pain) because their pain receptors cannot fire.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The anterolateral or spinothalamic system.
In this system, pain information crosses the midline immediately at the spinal cord level before ascending to the thalamus.
cards.back_text: The anterolateral or spinothalamic system. In this system, pain information crosses the midline immediately at the spinal cord level before ascending to the thalamus.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Glutamate and Substance P.
Glutamate acts as a primary neurotransmitter, while Substance P acts as a neuromodulator to enhance the pain signal.
cards.back_text: Glutamate and Substance P. Glutamate acts as a primary neurotransmitter, while Substance P acts as a neuromodulator to enhance the pain signal.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The cortical regions representing the arm and face expand into the area previously dedicated to the hand.
This is a form of neuroplasticity where neighboring sensory inputs take over the unused cortical territory.
cards.back_text: The cortical regions representing the arm and face expand into the area previously dedicated to the hand. This is a form of neuroplasticity where neighboring sensory inputs take over the unused cortical territory.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Touch crosses in the medulla (brainstem), while pain crosses in the spinal cord.
Both end up on the opposite side of the brain, but they decussate (cross) at different levels of the central nervous system.
cards.back_text: Touch crosses in the medulla (brainstem), while pain crosses in the spinal cord. Both end up on the opposite side of the brain, but they decussate (cross) at different levels of the central nervous system.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It can temporarily deplete pain fibers of their neurotransmitters.
After an initial burning sensation, the overstimulated pain fibers run out of chemicals to signal further pain.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It is a peptide neurotransmitter used to signal intense pain and trigger neural remodeling.
Substance P is released by nociceptors in response to intense stimuli. It promotes neural plasticity in postsynaptic neurons, which can affect long-term pain perception.
cards.back_text: It is a peptide neurotransmitter used to signal intense pain and trigger neural remodeling. Substance P is released by nociceptors in response to intense stimuli. It promotes neural plasticity in postsynaptic neurons, which can affect long-term pain perception.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Glutamate.
While Substance P is linked to intense pain, knockout mice lacking Substance P still respond to mild pain, suggesting glutamate carries those signals.
cards.back_text: Glutamate. While Substance P is linked to intense pain, knockout mice lacking Substance P still respond to mild pain, suggesting glutamate carries those signals.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
A somatosensory system that carries pain and temperature information from the body to the brain.
This system involves axons that cross the midline in the spinal cord before ascending to the thalamus and eventually the somatosensory cortex.
cards.back_text: A somatosensory system that carries pain and temperature information from the body to the brain. This system involves axons that cross the midline in the spinal cord before ascending to the thalamus and eventually the somatosensory cortex.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Pain signals project to the thalamus, while itch signals terminate in the pons.
Although both pathways cross the midline in the spinal cord, they report to different brain regions; itch specific fibers target the pons via the neurotransmitter Nppb.
cards.back_text: Pain signals project to the thalamus, while itch signals terminate in the pons. Although both pathways cross the midline in the spinal cord, they report to different brain regions; itch specific fibers target the pons via the neurotransmitter Nppb.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Natriuretic polypeptide B (Nppb).
Itch-specific fibers use Nppb to stimulate dorsal horn neurons, distinct from the glutamate and Substance P used by pain fibers.
cards.back_text: Natriuretic polypeptide B (Nppb). Itch-specific fibers use Nppb to stimulate dorsal horn neurons, distinct from the glutamate and Substance P used by pain fibers.
cards.front_text: What peptide neurotransmitter is specific to the reporting of itch to the spinal cord?

cards.back_html_sanitized:
Chronic pain caused by inappropriate signaling by neurons rather than ongoing tissue damage.
Neuropathic pain, such as phantom limb pain, occurs when the nervous system amplifies pain signals through maladaptive neural plasticity.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
They release chemicals that make dorsal horn neurons hyperexcitable.
Following nerve injury, microglia surround synapses in the spinal cord and increase the activity of pain-signaling neurons, flooding the brain with pain signals.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
The cingulate cortex.
The cingulate cortex, particularly the anterior portion, is activated by the distress of pain and even by social rejection or empathizing with others' pain.
cards.back_text: The cingulate cortex. The cingulate cortex, particularly the anterior portion, is activated by the distress of pain and even by social rejection or empathizing with others' pain.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It uses visual feedback to trick the brain into 'seeing' the missing limb move and relax.
The visual illusion of the missing limb being intact and moving can lead the brain to reinterpret painful signals from the stump as non-painful.
cards.back_text: It uses visual feedback to trick the brain into 'seeing' the missing limb move and relax. The visual illusion of the missing limb being intact and moving can lead the brain to reinterpret painful signals from the stump as non-painful.

cards.back_html_sanitized:
It signals damage in blood vessels and releases CGRP, causing the sensation of pain.
A wave of neuronal hyperexcitation (cortical spreading depression) stimulates the trigeminal nerve, leading to the release of the neuropeptide CGRP.
